sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Long agent traces composed of chains of thought and tool calls accumulate stale content that anchor subsequent generations, and eventually outgrow the context window. Existing scaffolds mitigate it with fixed-interval compaction triggered at a token threshold. Such triggers pay no heed to trajectory structure, risking discard of partial results mid-derivation or mid-search. We propose SelfCompact, a scaffold that allows the model itself to decide when and how to compact. Specifically, it pairs two inference-time elements: (i) a compaction tool the model invokes to summarize the accumulated context, and (ii) a lightweight rubric specifying when to fire (a sub-task has resolved, or the trajectory is converging) and when to suppress (mid-derivation, or when stuck). Both are needed. The tool alone is unevenly used across open-weight models, often invoked at unhelpful moments or not at all; the rubric alone cannot act. Together, they elicit effective adaptive compaction without any fine-tuning or external supervision. We present empirical results on six benchmarks (competitive math and agentic search) and seven models. Our results show that SelfCompact matches or exceeds fixed-interval summarization at a fraction of the token cost, improving over a no-summarization baseline by up to 18.1 points on math and 5-9 points on agentic search at 30-70% lower per-question cost. Our results expose a meta-cognitive gap: although unprompted models cannot reliably tell when their own context is rotting, a lightweight rubric closes this gap, reframing when to compact as a capability that scaffolds can supply without training.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: We are chasing after harder problems over longer horizons [METR, 2026], and consequently, the trajectories LMs generate to solve them keep growing. Reasoning models are now able to spend tens of thousands of tokens deliberating on single competition math question: Qwen3.5 produces 81k tokens [Qwen Team, 2026], Kimi-K2.5 produces 96k [Kimi Team et al., 2026]. Agentic systems extend further, orchestrating search results [Wei et al., 2025], code execution outputs [Jimenez et al., 2024], and intermediate plans [Novikov et al., 2025] across hundreds of turns. The bet that more thinking and interactions yields better answers has paid off, but long traces carry hidden cost. As the trace grows, it accumulates junk flawed case split made early, search result the model has moved past, candidate program that led nowhere. These leftovers do not just sit there; they anchor everything that follows [Laban et al., 2026]. model that solves problem from clean start often fails when fed back its own flawed reasoning. This phenomenon is known as context rot [Hong et al., 2025, Cheng et al., 2026]. Existing systems try to manage it with rigid rules: compacting the trajectory when token threshold is met [Cursor Research et al., 2026], or delegating the burden of identifying context rot to the user via /compact [Anthropic, 2025]. Preprint. Under Review. Figure 1: Comparison of trajectory-compression strategies on hard BrowseComp question. The gold answer requires verifying four facts (Agaricus, Bon 1983, Clash 1981, Harryhausen) before composing Medusa mushroom. Baseline (no compression) burns its budget on an unproductive monologue and emits no answer. Fixed-interval compression fires every two search trajectories regardless of reasoning state; the poorly-timed summary wipes verified facts mid-reasoning, leaving the model to guess (Morel Mushroom). SELFCOMPACT (Ours) gates compression on rubric over closed reasoning units, so each summary fires immediately after verified factall four facts persist and the model returns the correct answer. Today, this rigidity is the rule. Industrial deployed systems either wait until fixed token intervals to invoke compaction [Cursor Research et al., 2026]; recent academic scaffolds that compact context on reasoning traces [Wu et al., 2026a, Yan et al., 2026] and deep research systems [Chen et al., 2026], all rely on token threshold to trigger compaction. For example, Liu et al. [2025] triggers compaction when token usage exceeds 30% of the maximum context.1 But fixed threshold knows nothing about what the model is doing when it invokes summarization. It cannot distinguish model mid-derivation from one that has just resolved sub-problem, mid-search from one converging on an answer. The cost is asymmetric: well-timed call discards stale work, but poorly timed call discards the partial result the model needed to continue. These scaffolds help, but they leave substantial headroom. The right time to compact heavily depends on what the model is doing, not just on how many tokens have passed. Figure 1 shows this concretely: fixed-interval compression wipes four already-verified facts mid-trajectory, and the model falls back to guess. In this work, we ask the following research question: Can the LM agent itself recognize its own context rot without training, and compact accordingly? To answer the research question, we hand the decision of when to compact back to the LM agent itself. We propose SELFCOMPACT, scaffold that pairs two inference-time elements: an inline compaction tool the model invokes itself, and lightweight rubric specifying when to fire (a sub-task has resolved, or the trajectory is converging) and when to suppress (mid-derivation, or when stuck). The model emits <summarize> when it wants to compact, the scaffold runs the summarization, and generation resumes from the summarized prefix. Both the rubric judge and summarizer is the same model: no external verifier, no fine-tuning. 1https://huggingface.co/MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M2.5 2 Both elements are needed. Across seven open-weight models, four Qwen models on competition math and three deployed search agents (MiniMax-M2.5, GLM-4.7-Flash, MiMo-V2-Flash), ablating the rubric exposes uneven tool use: some models call the tool at unhelpful moments, others not at all. With the rubric in place, SELFCOMPACT elicits effective adaptive compaction without any fine-tuning, matching or exceeding fixed-interval summarization at fraction of the token cost: on competition math (IMO-Answerbench, HMMT Nov 25 / Feb 26), it improves over no-compaction baseline by up to 18.1 points; on agentic search (BrowseComp, BrowseComp-Plus, DeepSearch QA), it adds 59 points at 3070% lower per-question cost. To sum up, our contribution is three-fold: We propose SELFCOMPACT, novel scaffold that exposes summarization as an inline tool paired with lightweight rubric that tells the model when to invoke it. No fine-tuning required. Across seven open-weight models on competition math (four reasoning models) and agentic search (three deployed agents), SELFCOMPACT matches or exceeds fixed-interval summarization at substantially lower token cost. Ablations find that the rubric is crucial for effective self-compaction: offering the tool alone yields uneven behavior, while paragraph of guidance closes the gap. Our findings imply that knowing when to perform compaction can be supplied as meta-cognitive capability at inference time, rather than baked into weights. To the best of our knowledge, our work is the first to introduce and evaluate rubric-based adaptive compaction timing in training-free setting.

sections.title: 2 Preliminaries
sections.content: Context management in long-horizon agents. LM agents on long-horizon tasks accumulate tokens in single rolling context: prior thoughts, tool calls, and observations. single coding-agent session on SWE-bench-style tasks routinely spans millions of tokens and well over hundred turns. e.g., Qwen3-Coder-Next averages 8M tokens and 154 turns per problem on SWE-rebench [Badertdinov et al., 2026]. Deep-research agents on BrowseComp scale to hundreds of tool calls per query [Wei et al., 2025, MiroMind Team et al., 2026]. Even single-shot reasoning is no longer short: Kimi-K2.5 [Kimi Team et al., 2026] generates up to 96k thinking tokens on single competition math problem. As horizons and task difficulty continue to scale, this accumulated context is not merely expensive but actively harmful: model performance degrades sharply with length, phenomenon known as context rot [Hong et al., 2025]. Compaction is therefore prerequisite for sustained long-horizon capability. Current strategies. Todays agents compact via two content-agnostic heuristics. Reactive compaction triggers only when the rolling context approaches the models token budget, treating compaction purely as overflow prevention [Anthropic, 2025]. Periodic compaction fires on fixed intervalevery turns or every tokensregardless of what the trajectory contains [Cursor Research et al., 2026, Wu et al., 2026a]. Both ignore the state of the trajectory, and fail in opposite directions: reactive compaction waits until the context is already saturated with stale or erroneous tokens that have been polluting generation for many steps, while periodic compaction discards context indiscriminately, often summarizing in the middle of an active subgoal and erasing information the model still needs. Figure 1 illustrates how fixed-interval summary firing mid-reasoning on BrowseComp trajectory can wipe four already-verified facts, and the model is left to guess.

sections.title: 3 Our Approach: SELFCOMPACT
sections.content: Setup. Given natural language prompt x, language model π( x) generates continuation = (y1, y2, . . .) autoregressively, with each token conditioned on the prompt and all preceding tokens. We equip the language model with summarization tool : (x, y1:t) y, which takes the original prompt and (possibly partial) continuation y1:t and produces condensed version y. Generation then resumes from the new context (x, y). Crucially, the summarizer is the same model πno external verifier or auxiliary model is required. 3 Algorithm 1: SELFCOMPACT: rubric-gated inference-time context compaction. Input: prompt x; model π; probe interval ; step budget ; rubric prompt PR; summarizer prompt PS. Notation :C appends message to context C; π( C) samples response from π given C; pop(C, m) pop (removes) message from (assuming that its the last item in C). // Overall context and its KV cache 1 x; 2 for = 1, . . . , do 3 yt π( C); yt; if yt is final answer then return yt; if mod = 0 then PR; rt π( C), rt {COMPRESS, CONTINUE}; rt; if rt = COMPRESS then // rubric probe; KV prefix reused PS; π( C); y; else // KV prefix still reused // summarize trajectory // hard reset; resume from summary pop(C, rt); pop(C, PR); // revert; trajectory unchanged end 5 6 7 8 9 11 12 13 14 end 15 16 end 17 return yT ; SELFCOMPACT. SELFCOMPACT pairs two inference-time elements: (i) the summarization tool exposed to the model, and (ii) lightweight rubric that decides, at periodic probe intervals, whether should fire. Both elements are needed: exposing the tool aloneletting π emit <summarize> whenever it wantsyields uneven, often poorly-timed firing across models: some call the tool reflexively at unhelpful moments, others rarely at all. The rubric closes that gap by translating sub-task has resolved and the trajectory is not stuck mid-derivation into concrete, cite-able conditions π can verify against the trajectory in front of it. SELFCOMPACT runs on single inference engine with no fine-tuning. Summarizer design. To maximize KV-cache reuse, we implement by appending summarizer prompt (Box for Math; Box for Search Agents) as user-role message: the cache of y1:t at trigger time is preserved across the call, so pays prefill only on the appended instruction, not on the full trajectory. saving we quantify in the cost analysis below. The scaffold appends the instruction to (x, y1:t), π generates the summary y, and then replaces y1:t; decoding resumes from the original question and the summarized content (x, y). Triggering the summarizer. At intervals {N, 2N, 3N, . . .}, the scaffold appends rubric prompt PR as user-role message to (x, y1:t) and samples from π(x, y1:t, PR) binary verdict rt {COMPRESS, CONTINUE}. These intervals are measured in tokens of the reasoning trace (math) or in tool calls (agentic search). As with S, the probe is appended rather than substituted so that the KV cache of y1:t is preserved across the call, keeping the judge near-free relative to the trajectory length. The probe enumerates small set of conditions, each requiring verbatim evidence quoted from the trajectory, so π can check them locally. The rubric is task-specific: the conditions for competition math differ from those for agentic search, and we defer the exact wording to Appendix (math) and Appendix (search). On COMPRESS verdict, the summarizer fires: S(x, y1:t) whenever 0 (mod ) and rt = COMPRESS; otherwise generation resumes from (x, y1:t) unchanged. Workflow. Our experiments compare three regimes that share the same generation loop but differ in how the transcript is compressed: no compaction (the trajectory accumulates untruncated), 4 fixed-interval compaction (summarize-and-replace at every probe interval, and SELFCOMPACT (summarize-and-replace only when the rubric returns COMPRESS, otherwise continue). On agentic search this yields searchjudgesummarizesearch loop, with the agent continuing against rather than the full history after each compression. Cost analysis. Define = y1:t as the pre-compression trajectory length and ℓ = for the summary length, both in tokens. SELFCOMPACT adds at most two LLM calls per probe interval: the rubric probe always, the summarizer only on COMPRESS verdict. Both append to (x, y1:t), so the KV cache of y1:t is reused across the call and the O(L2) re-prefill that naive re-encode would incur is avoided. The rubric probe is essentially free: short verdict rt generated on top of the cached prefix. The summarizer is the only real overhead: O(Lℓ) to generate plus one-time O(ℓ2) prefill of the new (x, y) prefix. Empirically the trade is favorable: on agentic search SELFCOMPACT adds 59 accuracy points at 3070% lower per-question token cost than the no-compaction baseline (4.2); on competition math it leads on 11 of 12 benchmark/model cells under token budget matched to fixed-interval summarization (4.1). Appendix states the per-question cost formula in full, defines how (Nprefill, Ncached, Nout) are accumulated from each trajectory, lists the OpenRouter prices, and works out the cached-vs-prefill break-even (compaction wins iff L/ℓ > 10, which the search summarizer reaches at 2080).

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: 4.1 Multi-turn Reasoning in Competition Math Setup. We evaluate 4 models of different sizes in the Qwen family 2 instruct models Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507, and 2 Qwen3.5 [Qwen Team, 2026]: Qwen3.5-4B and Qwen3.5-9B, on 3 benchmarks: IMO-Answerbench [Luong et al., 2025], HMMT Nov 2025 and HMMT Feb 2026 [Balunovic et al., 2025]. We generate 16 responses for each question in parallel and report the mean across samples. We use vLLM [Kwon et al., 2023] for inference and the math_verify package for parsing and evaluation. We compare SELFCOMPACT against fixed interval summary, where summarization is always triggered for every 16k tokens, we keep making the model compact until it uses similar amount of tokens compared to SELFCOMPACT for fair comparison. We use the same scaffold as ReasoningCache [Wu et al., 2026a], prompting the model to continue generating based on an existing trajectory or summary of it. Detailed summarization and continuation prompts can be found in Appendix A. Results. Table 1 shows the results across three competition math benchmarks and four model configurations. Under matched token budgets, SELFCOMPACT consistently outperforms both the baseline and fixed interval summarization, achieving the best performance in 11 out of 12 settings. The gains are most pronounced for the thinking-enabled models: on Qwen3.5-9B, SELFCOMPACT improves over the baseline by 16.4, 10.0, and 18.1 points on IMO-Answerbench, HMMT Nov, and HMMT Feb respectively. The one exception is Qwen3-30B-A3B on HMMT Feb, where fixed interval summarization edges out SELFCOMPACTby 1.1 points, though the latter still leads on the other two benchmarks. Headroom analysis. We analyze the failure modes of fixed interval summarization for Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 on IMOAnswerbench by tracking answer transitions across the 12 summarization calls. Table 2 reports the number of instances where the models answer changes from correct to wrong after summarization and vice versa. Method Accuracy (%) No Compaction [16k] Fixed Interval Summary [44k] SELFCOMPACT [48k] Oracle (skip if correct) [44k] 38.91.7 41.42.1 45.52.8 52.91.4 Setup. We evaluate 3 models of different sizes and families: GLM-4.7-Flash (30B total, 3B active; [GLM Team et al., 2025]), MiniMax-M2.5 (230B total, 10B active; [MiniMax Team, 2026]) and Mimo-V2-Flash (309B total, 15B active; [MiMo Team et al., 2026]) on 3 agentic search tasks: BrowseComp [Wei et al., 2025] , BrowseComp plus [Chen et al., 2025] and DeepSearch QA [Gupta et al., 2026]. We use the exact same scaffold and evaluation settings as Lee et al. [2026]. We defer detailed sampling parameters and prompts to Appendix B. For cost, we report the per-question USD cost. In the main text we use single-rate approximation that bills every prompt token at the cached rate, COST(q) = (pcache Nprompt + pout Nout)/106, where Nprompt and Nout are the cumulative prompt and completion tokens summed across every LLM call in the trajectory (assistant turns, rubric probes, and summaries). This is conservative: the first-time prefill tokens that provider bills at the higher uncached rate pin are only small fraction (empirically 58%) of Nprompt, so the effective rate stays close to pcache. Prices come from OpenRouter  (Table 8)  . Appendix gives the full procedure, and Appendix reports the finer-grained accounting that separates first-time prefill (billed at pin) from cached reads (billed at pcache). We compare SELFCOMPACT against the following baseline methods: No Compaction: No context management is used, model stops when either max context window is reached, or max number of tool calls are executed. Fixed-interval summary: Summarization is triggered when 30% of the max context window is consumed, threshold suggested by Liu et al. [2025], closely resembling Wu et al. [2026a]. Delete-all: All the past trajectory is discarded when 30% of the max context window is consumed. 6 Keep-last-N: The last 3 turns are saved and the rest is discarded when 30% of the max context window is consumed. Main Results. Table 4 shows the results of the three deployed agents (GLM-4.7-Flash, MiniMax-M2.5, MiMo-V2-Flash) on agentic search. SELFCOMPACT is the strongest method for all three models, improving over the no-compaction baseline by +8.5 (GLM-4.7-Flash), +9.2 (MiniMax-M2.5), and +5.3 (Mimo-V2-Flash) absolute points on BrowseComp-Plus. The accuracy ordering is consistent across all three models: baseline < fixed-interval SELFCOMPACT. Fixed-interval summarization recovers most of the baselines lost ground; SELFCOMPACTs autonomous trigger adds up to +6.3 points by avoiding poorly-timed compressions the rubric concentrates that distribution at meaningful stopping points. On the cost axis, SELFCOMPACT is cheaper than the baseline despite issuing an extra LLM call at each probe. Per-question cost on BrowseComp-Plus drops by 67% (GLM-4.7-Flash, 0.12 0.04), 63% (MiniMax-M2.5, 0.19 0.07), and 33% (Mimo-V2-Flash, 0.24 0.16). The KV-cache reuse described in 3 is what makes the probe affordable: each rubric judgement contributes only its own short generation, since the trajectorys cache is preserved. We plot the number of tokens accumulated before each compaction fires in Figure 2, comparing Fixed-Interval (30% of context) against SELFCOMPACT on BrowseComp-Plus. SELFCOMPACTs rubric-fired summaries skew to the left of the 30% threshold line in all three models, whereas Fixed-Interval pins triggers at the threshold by construction. Bars compare Baseline (no compression), fixed-budget Threshold (30% of context), and SELFCOMPACT. SELFCOMPACT matches the others on easy bins and pulls ahead on the hardest bins across all three models. SELFCOMPACT helps on harder problems. We bin each question by the total output tokens its no-compression baseline trajectory consumed (a model-specific proxy for question difficulty) and split into five equal-size quantiles per model. Within each bin we report accuracy for the baseline, fixed-budget Threshold (30% of context), and SELFCOMPACT (Figure 3). On easy bins the three policies are within sampling noise, but on the two hardest bins SELFCOMPACT improves over the Threshold baseline by 520 pp consistently across 3 models, indicating that rubric-driven compression helps the most on questions that require deep search and accumulate the largest contexts. Ablation on the effect of rubrics. We ablate the effect of rubrics by removing the rubric-gated verification and instead letting the model self-decide whether to summarize at each step. This collapses performance to 41.0% on average with GLM-4.7-Flash  (Table 5)  , 5.4-point drop from full SelfCompact (46.4%) that leaves it on par with the naive fixed-interval schedule (41.5%) and below it on BrowseComp (33.6 vs 35.4), with the same effect on math reasoning using Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 (40.9% vs 45.5%, Table 6). This shows that the gains do not come from triggering compaction per se, but from the rubric-gated verification that determines what reasoning state is safe to preserve; without it, the models unconstrained summarization discards information 8 Table 5: Accuracy (%) across agentic search benchmarks with GLM-4.7-Flash. Subscripts denote standard deviation over 5 runs. Best per column in bold.

sections.title: 5 Related Work
sections.content: Context compaction in frontier models. In API-based models, context compaction has become standard built-in feature to prevent multi-rounds of long reasoning chains from blowing up the context window.2 Note that while the feature is often named as auto compaction, it refers to the compaction operation being automatically triggered and performed when the context length reaches target threshold, i.e. fixed interval. Such mechanisms have raised complaints from the community3, where compaction at inappropriate positions tends to remove important information from the context, which is central issue we aim to resolve. The only exception is new feature from LangChain posted concurrently to our work4 March 2026, where they also introduced summary tool; they did not introduce the rubric-based criteria for deciding when to summarize. Method Table 6: Accuracy (%) on IMOBench with Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507. Subscripts denote standard deviation. Learning to compact during post-training. Longhorizon reasoning and agentic search accumulate tokens at an unsustainable rate: observations pile up, intermediate results go stale, and prior context anchors rather than informs subsequent generations. broad line of work addresses this through periodic compaction, sharing common paradigm: generate an extended sequence of thoughts or actions, summarize the accumulated context, resume from the compressed state, and repeat [Shao et al., 2025, Sun et al., 2025]. The majority of these methods train models to perform compaction (via SFT, RL, or both) in order to encode the when-to-compact decision implicitly in teacher demonstrations or rewards [Kontonis et al., 2026, Yang et al., 2026, Yan et al., 2026, Cassano and Rush, 2026, Mitra and Ulukus, 2025, Aghajohari et al., 2025, Wu et al., 2026a, Sun et al., 2025, Ye et al., 2025, Shao et al., 2025, Wu et al., 2026b]. Our approach is entirely training-free. We draw on recent advances in tool-using models [GLM Team et al., 2025, MiniMax Team, 2026], which have unlocked the ability to invoke external tools mid-generation, and show that off-the-shelf models can self-compact effectively if scaffolded correctly. lightweight rubric specifying when to invoke the compaction tool is sufficient to elicit reliable adaptive compaction without any parameter updates. No Compaction Fixed-interval SELFCOMPACT w/o rubrics 38.91.7 41.42.1 40.92. SELFCOMPACT 45.52.8 Math Across these methods, most also trigger compaction at fixed token-count thresholds independent of trajectory structure [Yan et al., 2026, Aghajohari et al., 2025, Wu et al., 2026a,b], risking compaction mid-derivation when the model still needs the context it is about to discard. Our rubric is designed precisely to detect these structural conditions, firing when sub-task has resolved or the trajectory is converging, and suppressing when the model is mid-derivation or stuck. Few works have studied compaction without model training. The closest to ours is by Zhu et al. [2026a], which introduces training-free variant that uses frontier model as an external summarizer applied at fixed intervals after each full search trajectory. Our work differs in that, we introduce rubric that dynamically determines when compaction should fire rather than relying on fixed post2e.g. https://platform.claude.com/cookbook/tool-use-automatic-context-compaction 3https://www.reddit.com/r/codex/comments/1qib69i/auto_compaction_is_not_that_helpful/ 4https://www.langchain.com/blog/autonomous-context-compression 9 trajectory intervals. Our work also connects to broader goal of context management. We refer the interested reader to the existing surveys on this topic [Mei et al., 2025]. KV Cache Eviction. complementary line of work reduces memory and compute costs by evicting or compressing KV cache entries during inference. Static methods retain tokens based on recency [Xiao et al., 2023] or attention scores [Li et al., 2024], while more recent approaches use richer importance measures that are often learned [Park et al., 2025, Eyuboglu et al., 2025, Zweiger et al., 2026, Monea et al., 2025, Li et al., 2026]. Across this literature, interpretability of the compressed representation is treated as secondary: what is retained or evicted need not be human-readable, only attention-efficient, and may even be complemented by black-box weight updates [Zuo et al., 2026]. The context compaction literature (which is where our work fits in), by contrast, prioritizes discrete natural-language summaries that remain inspectable and overridden.

sections.title: 6 Limitations
sections.content: Model capability gap. We only evaluated open-weight models; frontier systems (GPT-5.5 [OpenAI, 2026], Claude Opus 4.7 [Anthropic, 2026], Gemini 3 Pro [Google, 2026]) report stronger metacognition [Lindsey, 2025] and may detect context rot without rubric. SELFCOMPACT is complementary: training-free scaffold that layers onto any model and closes the gap precisely where it exists in deployed open-weight agents. No reinforcement learning. We scope this work to training-free interventions to isolate the rubrics contribution. Prior work [Kontonis et al., 2026, Zhu et al., 2026b] shows RL can teach model both when and what to compact. We view RL as natural extension: the rubric supplies behavioral target that RL can distill into the policy.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced SELFCOMPACT, rubric-gated summarization scaffold that compacts agent trajectories at closed reasoning units rather than on fixed intervals. Across six benchmarks and seven models, it matches or exceeds fixed-interval summarization at 3070% lower cost, reframing when to compact as capability that lightweight rubric can supply without training.

sections.title: A Math experimental setup
sections.content: Models. We evaluate four open-weight Qwen models on competition math: two instruction-tuned models (Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507) and two thinkingdisabled variants (Qwen3.5-4B and Qwen3.5-9B). All checkpoints are loaded from Hugging Face and served via vLLM [Kwon et al., 2023]. The 30B-A3B model uses tensor-parallel size 2; all others run on single H200 GPU. Benchmarks. February 2026. Final answers are extracted and scored with the math_verify package. IMO-Answerbench [Luong et al., 2025], HMMT November 2025, and HMMT Sampling parameters. We use temperature 1.0 and top-p 0.7 for all models, generating = 16 samples per question and reporting mean std over samples. Each generation round is capped at 16,384 tokens (num_tokens 16384); the per-trajectory round budget is max_rounds 12, and the summarizer output is hard-truncated at 512 tokens (max_summary_tokens 512). Scaffold. We adopt the ReasoningCache scaffold of Wu et al. [2026a]: at each round, the model is prompted to either solve from scratch or improve on summary of the previous attempt. The continuation prompt issued to the model after every refinement is reproduced verbatim in Box A. Methods. BASELINE runs the model with single 16k-token budget (max_rounds 1) and no summarization. FIXED-INTERVAL SUMMARY sets force_tool_calls, which fires the summarizer at the end of every round (i.e. every 16,384 generated tokens) regardless of trajectory state. SELFCOMPACT exposes <tool_call> as vLLM stop token so that the model autonomously emits the summarizer; the round ends and the summary fires whenever the stop token appears. SELFCOMPACT W/ RUBRICS interposes the rubric probe in Box between rounds: if the rubric fires COMPRESS the summarizer is invoked, otherwise the model continues from the unmodified prefix. Math rubric prompt (ANSWER / STUCK / HAS-NEXT; appended to (x, y1:t) at every round boundary) Judge your math-solving state from the conversation above. Answer Q1. . . Q3 with or and one short evidence phrase. Answers without evidence must be N. Non-trivial fact = new equation, reduction, bound, case-elimination, or counterexample, not paraphrase. Q1 ANSWER: The latest round states specific final answer (a boxed{} expression or Final Answer: . . . ), not merely partial result. If Y, quote the answer verbatim. If N, state what is still unknown. Q2 STUCK: Your last 2 rounds added no non-trivial fact only paraphrases or abandoned attempts. If Y, name the two rounds and write no new fact. If N, name one non-trivial fact from the last 2 rounds. Q3 HAS-NEXT: You can write the exact next step (case split, substitution, verification, lemma to prove). If Y, write the step as one imperative sentence. If N, write NONE. Output: exactly 3 lines, no preamble or trailing text. Q1: Y/N -- <evidence> Q2: Y/N -- <evidence> Q3: Y/N -- <evidence> Fire rule: COMPRESS iff Q1 = (Q2 = Q3 = Y). Branch (Q1 = Y) fires lock-in re-prompt that preserves the boxed answer; Branch (Q2 = Q3 = Y) fires reset that preserves the named next step. 16 Math summarizer prompt (preserve-answer v3; replaces y1:t with when fired) Context Refinement Prompt: Original Prompt: {original_prompt} Partial Generation: {partial_generation} Your task is to create compressed summary for another model to continue solving from. RULES: 1. If final answer (e.g., boxed{}) was found, PRESERVE IT at the end of your summary. 2. Keep key insights, important calculations, and the reasoning path. 3. Remove redundant text, false starts, and unnecessary repetition. 4. If the answer seems wrong or unverified, note that verification is needed. 5. Be concise but preserve all critical mathematical steps. Output format: Key insights and progress made. Important intermediate results. If found: Final Answer: [the answer] or the boxed{} expression. If not solved: what still needs to be done. Continuation prompt (issued to π at the start of every round, with filled in if available) You are given maths problem. You may also be given summary of previous attempt to solve it. This previous attempt may or may not be correct. ### PROBLEM {problem} ### SUMMARY OF PREVIOUS ATTEMPT {summary} ### INSTRUCTIONS If no summary of previous attempt is provided, solve the problem from scratch. If summary of previous attempt is provided, your task is to improve upon this attempt. You should rely on this summary to guide your thinking. Some strategies you could use include: Verifying the previous solution. Proving the result in different way. Finding alternative problem-solving strategies. Continuing from where the previous solution left off, assuming that the previous solution is incomplete. Reason step-by-step and return your final answer in boxed{}. 17 Agentic-search experimental setup Models and serving. The MiniMax-M2.5, Mimo-V2-Flash) are accessed through OpenRouter via the AggAgent runtime [Lee et al., 2026]. We use the unmodified react_agent_selfcheck scaffold; the rubric and summarizer prompts are reproduced verbatim in Box and Box B. three deployed agents (GLM-4.7-Flash, Benchmarks. BrowseComp [Wei et al., 2025], BrowseComp-Plus [Chen et al., 2025], and DeepSearchQA [Gupta et al., 2026]. Following Sun et al. [2025] and Lee et al. [2026], we sub-sample 150 questions per benchmark. Sampling parameters. All three models share the same decoding configuration: temperature 1.0, top-p 0.95, max output tokens per call 10,000, parallel-tool-calls disabled, and per-trajectory cap of 100 LLM calls. Per-model context windows, the 30%-of-context trigger used by FIXED-INTERVAL SUMMARY and the rubrics token-pct backstop, and model-specific decoding flags are listed in Table 7. Table 7: Per-model context windows, 30%-of-context summarization trigger, and decoding flags for the three OpenRouter agents. GLM-4.7-Flash keeps the thinking trace visible to the rubric probe via enable_thinking=True and clear_thinking=False. Model Ctx window 30% trigger Model-specific flags GLM-4.7-Flash MiniMax-M2.5 Mimo-V2-Flash 128,000 196,608 262, 38,400 58,982 78,643 enable_thinking=True, clear_thinking=False reasoning_split=True (default OpenRouter body) Scaffold details. single trajectory is the standard ReAct loop: the agent emits tool call (search and either visit for BrowseComp/DeepSearchQA or get_document_bcp for BrowseComp-Plus), receives the result, reasons, and either issues another tool call or commits final answer. For BrowseComp and DeepSearchQA, search is backed by the Serper API (https://serper.dev/) and visit fetches and extracts page content via crawl4ai (https://github.com/unclecode/crawl4ai); for BrowseComp-Plus, search and get_document_bcp resolve against the benchmarks released corpus. The rubric in Box is appended to copy of the trajectory at each round boundary so the probe does not pollute the rolling cache. COMPRESS fires only when four gates pass simultaneously: (ROUND) iteration 3, (TOKENS) running prompt length 40,000, (CAP) total summaries so far < 1, and (PERIOD) 2 rounds elapsed since the last probe. The token-pct backstop forces COMPRESS once the prompt crosses 0.30 ctx_window regardless of the rubric. 18 Search rubric probe (appended to (x, y1:t) at every round boundary) You are about to decide whether to compress your conversation history into summary that REPLACES the full history above. After compression, you continue research from only [system, original_question, assistant_summary, user_continue]. Compression is irreversible: anything not preserved in the summary is gone. Compression is safe ONLY when ALL FOUR of the following hold: (C1) the trajectory has reached closed unit (not mid-thought), (C2) the essential information is reducible to 35 cite-able facts without loss, (C3) something has progressed since the last compression, (N1) you are NOT currently stuck in way summarization would mask. Answer C1, C2, C3, N1 honestly. Each answer requires verbatim evidence quoted from the trajectory above; answers without evidence default to N. C1 CLOSED-UNIT: The most recent assistant message is closed unit completed tool call whose result is now visible, or completed sub-analysis with clear stopping point. It is NOT mid-sentence reasoning (Let me now check. . . , should next look at. . . ), and not half-formulated query. If Y, quote the closing fragment of the last assistant message. If N, quote the open fragment that shows the trajectory is mid-thought. C2 SUMMARIZABLE: You can write 35 essential facts (with verbatim citations from the trajectory) that future-you needs to continue research after compression. Each fact must be single concrete statement: name, date, URL, quoted claim, or resolved sub-question. Answer if the trajectorys value is dispersed across many small inferences (e.g., list of dead-end queries needed to avoid retries, negative results that constrain hypothesis space) that would be lost without the dispersal. If Y, list the 35 facts numbered, each with verbatim citation in quotes, separated by . If N, name in one sentence the class of information that would be lost. C3 PROGRESS: Since the most recent compression (or since the start of the conversation if none), you have either obtained new concrete fact (name, date, URL, or quoted claim) OR refined the sub-question being pursued. If Y, name the new fact or refined sub-question. If N, state that you are returning the same state you compressed from. N1 STUCK: At least 3 of your last 4 search queries returned no new URL or fact (i.e., were duplicates or returned already-known content). If you have made fewer than 4 searches total, answer N. If Y, name 1 distinct strategy you have NOT yet tried (different tool, different query type, different angle on the question). If N, name one new URL or fact obtained recently. Output: exactly 4 lines, no preamble or trailing text. C1: Y/N -- <evidence> C2: Y/N -- <if Y: 1. fact "citation" 2. fact "citation" 3. fact "citation"; if N: <class of info lost>> C3: Y/N -- <evidence> N1: Y/N -- <evidence> Fire rule: COMPRESS iff C1 = C2 = C3 = N1 = N; otherwise CONTINUE. Summarizer. The search summarizer is the webresummer variant of Wu et al. [2026b], reproduced verbatim in Box below; we use it unmodified for all three deployed agents. 19 Summarizer prompt (S instruction appended to (x, y1:t)) You are an expert at analyzing conversation history and extracting relevant information. Your task is to thoroughly evaluate the conversation history above and the users original question to provide summary that will REPLACE the full conversation history when you continue. Task Guidelines 1. Information Analysis: Carefully analyze the conversation history to identify truly useful information. Focus on information that directly contributes to answering the question. Do NOT make assumptions, guesses, or inferences beyond what is explicitly stated in the conversation. If information is missing or unclear, do NOT include it in your summary. 2. Summary Requirements: Extract only the most relevant information that is explicitly present in the conversation. Synthesize information from multiple exchanges when relevant. Only include information that is certain and clearly stated in the conversation. Do NOT output or mention any information that is uncertain, insufficient, or cannot be confirmed from the conversation.

sections.title: C Cost analysis of summarization
sections.content: Cost model (single-rate). question with the additive token-cost In the main text, for all OpenRouter-hosted agents we charge each COST(q) = (cid:16) 1 106 pcache Nprompt + pout Nout (cid:17) , where Nprompt is the cumulative prompt tokens summed across every LLM call in the trajectory assistant turns, the rubric probe, and the summarizer call, and Nout is the cumulative completion tokens over the same set of calls. We charge every prompt token at the cache rate pcache rather than separately tracking which tokens were cache hit and which were first-time prefill. This is conservative single-rate approximation: the first-time prefill tokens the question, each assistant turn, and each tool observation, counted once when they first enter the prefix are only small fraction (empirically 58%) of Nprompt, which counts each token once per call it survives, so the effective per-token rate sits close to pcache even when provider-side caching misses occasionally. Appendix relaxes this approximation, splitting Nprompt into first-time prefill Nprefill (billed at the uncached rate pin) and cache reads Ncached (billed at pcache) and reporting the resulting two-rate cost in Table 9. Per-1M token prices are listed in Table 8. Calculation procedure. For each of the 150 sampled questions per benchmark, we (i) load the trajectorys per-call usage records logged by the AggAgent runtime, (ii) accumulate (Nprompt, Nout) as defined above, (iii) apply the models prices from Table 8, and (iv) report the mean across the 150 questions in Table 4 (units: USD per question). Table 8: OpenRouter prices in USD per 1,000,000 tokens. Model GLM-4.7-Flash MiMo-V2-Flash MiniMax-M2.5 pin 0.07 0.10 0.30 pcache 0.01 0.01 0.03 pout 0.40 0.30 1.20 Where compaction saves cost. Under the single-rate cache billing above, the savings from compaction come entirely from shortening every subsequent calls prompt. After fire that collapses -token prefix to t-token summary, every later call pays pcache instead of pcache , /t ratio per call. Empirically summarization collapses 50100k trajectory into 13k, giving 2080 shrinkage of the post-compact prompt. The rubric probe and summarizer both append to the live prefix so the running prompt is preserved during the probe itself; the probe contributes only its own 60-token verdict to Nout, and the summarizer contributes tokens once, both negligible relative to the future-call savings amortized across the rest of the trajectory. Math. The Qwen runs are served locally with vLLM, so cost collapses to wall-clock trade-off rather than dollar one. We report the per-question budget tag [Xk] in Table 1: the average sum of generated tokens (rounds num_tokens) plus refinement tokens across the = 16 samples. FIXED-INTERVAL SUMMARYs budget is matched to SELFCOMPACT within 3k tokens so all four rows in each model block compete at identical compute.

sections.title: D Trajectory token consumption
sections.content: 1 Token lifecycle. trajectory under SELFCOMPACT looks like A1 A2 Ak 2 , where is the original question (plus system prompt), each At is one assistant turn (model output for tool call or final answer), and is summary the model emits when the rubric fires COMPRESS. Each token model produces is billed three different ways over its lifetime: Output when the model first emits the tokens (one charge). Prefill (uncached input) on the immediately next LLM call, when the tokens are appended to the prefix and seen by the provider for the first time (one charge). Cached input on every subsequent LLM call until the prefix is reset (one charge per reuse). Concretely, single At at round in trajectory of rounds contributes once to Output, once to Prefill, and times to Cached. summary emitted at compaction is billed once as output, once as prefill on the first post-compact call, then as cached input until the next compaction (or end of trajectory). Per-question token totals. Table 9 reports, for each (model, benchmark, method) cell, the cumulative prompt tokens Nprompt and completion tokens Nout summed across all LLM calls (assistant turns, rubric probes, summarizer calls), together with the split of Nprompt into first-time prefill Nprefill and cache reads Ncached. The single-rate cost reported in the main text  (Table 4)  recovers directly from the Nprompt column as (pcache Nprompt + pout Nout)/106. The Cost column of Table 9 instead applies the finer two-rate billing, (pin Nprefill + pcache Ncached + pout Nout)/106, charging the first-time prefill at the higher uncached rate pin; this raises each entry modestly above the single-rate value in Table 4 but preserves the headline comparison that SELFCOMPACT costs less than the no-compaction baseline. 22 Table 9: Per-question token accounting across all three benchmarks, splitting the cumulative prompt Nprompt into first-time prefill (Nprefill) and cache reads (Ncached), alongside output (Nout). sections.title: E Qualitative trajectories
sections.content: We walk through three BrowseComp-Plus questions on MiniMax-M2.5, each run two ways: fixedinterval compaction (summarize whenever the prompt passes 30% of the context window) and SELFCOMPACT (summarize only when the rubric judges that sub-task has closed). On all three questions both fixed-interval and the no-compaction baseline answer incorrectly, while SELFCOMPACT answers correctly so the difference is when compaction fires, not whether it fires. How to read these traces. Each turn is one search or get_document call. Fixed-interval compacts on blind clock: every time the running prompt refills to 60k tokens (30% of MiniMax-M2.5s window) it summarizes whatever is currently in context including dead-end searches. SELFCOMPACT probes rubric at intervals and compacts only when sub-task has genuinely resolved, so it can let the prompt grow well past 30% while the agent is still exploring. We write each compaction as (prefix tokens summary tokens). Case A: Whitesnake fixed-interval stays anchored, SELFCOMPACT breaks out Question. Name the band formed between 1970 and 1990 by musician who played in rock band that sold 100M+ records, began performing as teenager, was married three times, has one daughter and one son, attended art college, and worked in boutique. Gold: Whitesnake (the musician is David Coverdale, ex-Deep Purple). Fixed-interval @ 30% (wrong: Paul McCartney formed Wings). Turns 120. Cycles famous British rockers against the clues Keith Richards, Pete Townshend, Malcolm McLaren, Bryan Ferry none of whom fit. Compactions at turns 21, 41, 61, 82 (each 60k 13k). Each summary re-dumps the same ruled-out-names search log, so the agent resumes from the identical dead-end shortlist. Turns 22100. Keeps re-trying the same pool (Brian May, Mick Jagger, Ronnie Wood, Paul McCartney), never tests David Coverdale, hits the 100-search cap, and falls back to generic guess. SELFCOMPACT (correct: Whitesnake). Turns 140. The same broad cycling (Freddie Mercury, Bowie, Boy George, Stevie Nicks); the rubric keeps continuing no sub-task has closed and lets the prompt grow to 118k. One compaction at turn 41 (118.6k 1.1k), distilling the constraints rather than the ruled-out shortlist. Turns 4657. No longer re-primed with the old names, the agent tries fresh candidate David Coverdale confirms Deep Purple (100M+ records), art college, and the boutique job, and answers Whitesnake. Fixed-interval compacted four times but kept re-seeding the same wrong shortlist; SELFCOMPACTs single late compaction cleared it and let the agent escape to the right musician. 24 Case B: Majida El Roumi fixed-interval freezes wrong lead Question. An artist released their first solo album between 1975 and 1978, served as UN Goodwill Ambassador, and made song (released 20042007) that is rendition of 1956 1959 classical piece; different artist who started singing at age eight and cited Barbra Streisand and Queen released version between 1994 and 1997. Name the first artist. Gold: Majida El Roumi (her Habibi is built on Albinonis Adagio). Fixed-interval @ 30% (wrong: None). Turns 120. Chases both threads but latches onto the wrong classical piece Rachmaninoff, via Céline Dions All by Myself. Compactions at turns 21, 41, 61, 81 (60k each). Every summary carries the All by Myself lead forward, so the agent stays on Western pop (Nana Mouskouri, Eric Carmen, Cyndi Lauper, Angélique Kidjo). Turns 22100. Never reconsiders the piece or tests an Arabic artist; hits the cap and returns None. SELFCOMPACT (correct: Majida El Roumi). Turns 123. Searches both threads and at turns 1820 corrects the piece Albinonis Adagio, not Rachmaninoff then looks for an Adagio cover by UN-Ambassador artist. One compaction at turn 24 (70.2k 0.9k), consolidating the corrected lead. Turns 2542. Zeroes in on Majida El Roumi and her song Habibi, confirms the album dates, and answers correctly. The fixed clock froze an early wrong guess into every summary; SELFCOMPACT compacted only after the agent had corrected the lead to Albinonis Adagio. Case C: Raheem Sterling fixed-interval never reaches the match Question. league football match (19922023) in European capital, attendance 61,700 61,906; Team scored in the 6th minute (a left-footed player), Team scored in the 35th and 75th minutes and in stoppage time (90+4, 90+7). Name the player who assisted the third goal (the 75th-minute one). Gold: Raheem Sterling (Tottenham 14 Chelsea, 6 Nov 2023). Fixed-interval @ 30% (wrong: Unable to determine). Turns 122. Searches the goal-minute fingerprint (6/35/75/90+4/90+7) and the attendance band; the literal-minute queries return nothing relevant. Guesses SevillaRoma. Compactions at turns 23, 45, 67, 88 (60k each). Each summary re-dumps the same fruitless minute-by-minute log, and the agent keeps trying stadiums and teams at random (Stadio Olimpico, PSG, Man Utd, Moscow). Turns 24100. Never tests TottenhamChelsea; hits the cap and gives up. SELFCOMPACT (correct: Raheem Sterling). Turns 122. The same fingerprint searches, also unproductive. Turns 2325. Tries the right pairing Tottenham vs Chelsea, 41, November 2023 and hits the match. One compaction at turn 28 (70k 0.8k), consolidating the identified match; turns 2941 confirm the attendance, Kulusevskis left-footed 6th-minute goal, and Sterlings assist on the 75th-minute goal. Both arms flailed on the goal-minute fingerprint, but SELFCOMPACT kept exploring until the match was found and compacted only then; the fixed clock kept interrupting to re-summarize dead ends and never reached the pairing.
